title: Agile
lead: Iterative, value-driven delivery approach used to shape both the design process and the target operating model.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: Agile is a philosophy and set of practices for delivering value iteratively and incrementally. Originating in software development (Agile Manifesto, 2001), it has evolved into a broad operating approach that shapes how teams are structured, how work is planned, and how organisations deliver change. Within the ITOM context, Agile underpins the design approach itself — ITOM engagements are run iteratively — and is often embedded in the target operating model as a foundational way of working.

sections.heading: ITOM Perspective
sections.body: Agile methodologies serve as a foundational approach within the IT Operating Model for delivering value iteratively. When an organisation adopts an Agile-oriented ITOM, it shifts from project-based delivery to continuous product-centric flow. This affects team topology (cross-functional product teams), governance (lightweight steering, OKRs over gate reviews), toolchain (boards, backlogs, CI/CD), and culture (psychological safety, experimentation).

items.desc: work in short cycles (sprints or flows), validate regularly, adapt based on feedback
stable, empowered teams own outcomes end-to-end, reducing hand-offs
shift from project to product thinking; teams own a service or capability continuously
regular retrospectives and reviews drive continuous improvement at team and system level
lightweight planning, rolling roadmaps, and value-stream funding replace annual budgets and stage gates
the most widely used Agile framework; sprints, ceremonies, roles (PO, SM, team)
flow-based work management; visualise, limit WIP, manage flow
Scaled Agile Framework for large programmes and portfolio management
Large Scale Scrum; minimal scaffolding for multi-team product development
